sections.title: Biological Overview
content: Bulbasaur is a symbiotic species featuring a nutrient-rich bulb on its back. This bulb is planted at birth and grows in tandem with the Pokémon.
It can survive for days without food, relying solely on solar energy stored within its bulb through photosynthesis.

sections.title: Design Origins
content: Inspired by the Dicynodont, an extinct synapsid, combined with characteristics of common frogs.
Its name is a portmanteau of the English words 'Bulb' and the Greek 'Saurus' (Lizard).

sections.title: GBL Tactical History
content: A dominant contender in the Little Cup format.
The combination of Vine Whip's high energy generation and the legacy move Frenzy Plant makes it one of the most efficient Grass-type attackers in the meta.

sections.title: Terminal Data: Anomalies
content: Bulbasaur is the only dual-type starter in the Kanto region.
It holds the first index entry (#001) in the National Pokédex.

sections.title: Maturation Profile
content: As the bulb on its back matures into a bud, Ivysaur's weight increases, forcing it to develop stronger leg muscles.
When the bud is close to blooming, it emits a distinctively sweet aroma and the Pokémon begins spending significantly more time basking in direct sunlight.

sections.title: Historical Media Logs
content: Famously documented in the 'Bulbasaur's Mysterious Garden' logs, which show groups of the species gathering to evolve synchronously under moonlight.

sections.title: Apex Flora
content: The bulb has fully flowered into a massive bloom capable of absorbing large amounts of solar energy to power devastating beam attacks.
The scent from its flower is known to calm human emotions and can attract other Pokémon from miles away.

sections.title: GBL Tactical Analysis
content: A premiere choice for the Ultra League. Its Shadow variant is particularly feared for its ability to overwhelm Water-types with raw Frenzy Plant pressure.

sections.title: Thermal Core
content: Charmander's health is tied directly to the flame on its tail. If the flame flickers weakly, its health is declining; if it goes out, the Pokémon's life ends.
Steam is known to rise from the tip of its tail if it is caught in the rain.

sections.title: Mythological Baseline
content: Derived from the 'Fire Salamander' of European myth, which was believed to be capable of surviving inside active furnaces.

sections.title: Aggression Surge
content: Evolution triggers a surge in adrenaline and aggression. Charmeleon is highly territorial and will seek out opponents far larger than itself.
In battle, it exhales bluish-white flames, raising local temperatures to hazardous levels.

sections.title: System Designation: FLAME POKEMON
content: Charizard represents the final biological maturation of the Kanto Fire-type lineage. It is characterized by its powerful wings and a thermal core that powers a perpetual flame at the tip of its tail.
Despite its destructive potential, Charizard adheres to a primitive code of honor: it will never direct its full heat toward an opponent it perceives as significantly weaker than itself.

sections.title: Morphological Inspiration
content: While its English designation suggests a 'Charred Lizard,' its physiology is more closely aligned with the European 'Western Dragon'—a winged, fire-breathing reptilian apex predator.
Its Japanese name, 'Lizardon,' highlights its lizard-like ancestry over its draconic appearance.

sections.title: GBL Tactical Analysis
content: In the Go Battle League (GBL), Charizard has evolved from a niche pick to a premier meta threat. Its utility shifted dramatically following the 'Wing Attack' energy-generation buff and the introduction of the legacy move 'Blast Burn'.

sections.title: Terminal Data: Anomalies
content: STAT CLONE: Charizard shares the exact base stat distribution with the Johto starter, Typhlosion.
DUAL EVOLUTION: It is one of only two known species capable of two distinct Mega Evolutions (X and Y).

sections.title: Hydraulic Defense
content: Squirtle's shell is not just for protection; its rounded shape and surface grooves help minimize resistance in water, enabling the Pokémon to swim at high speeds.
When threatened, it withdraws into its shell and sprays water from its mouth with high pressure.

sections.title: Maturation and Longevity
content: The long, furry tail is a symbol of longevity. As it ages, scratches may appear on its shell as evidence of its toughness as a battler.
It is popular as a pet because its tail is considered to bring good luck.

sections.title: Hydraulic Artillery
content: Blastoise features pressurized water cannons inside its shell. These cannons can punch through thick steel and are accurate enough to hit small targets from over 160 feet away.
It deliberately makes itself heavy to withstand the recoil of the water jets it fires.

sections.title: GBL Tactical Analysis
content: Functions as a bulky Water-type closer in the Great League. Hydro Cannon is its primary win condition.

sections.title: Defense Mechanisms
content: Caterpie releases a powerful, stench-filled odor from its bright red antenna when it feels threatened. This serves to repel bird Pokémon predators.
Its feet are tipped with suction pads that allow it to scale vertical surfaces like trees.

sections.title: Electrical Capacitance
content: Pikachu stores electricity in the red pouches on its cheeks. When it releases this energy, it can cause small lightning bolts to strike.
It raises its tail to check its surroundings; it is occasionally struck by lightning in this position.

sections.title: Global Iconography
content: Pikachu was not originally intended to be the series mascot; the role was initially planned for Clefairy.

sections.title: Mystical Longevity
content: Ninetales is said to live for a thousand years. Each of its nine tails is imbued with a different supernatural power.
It is highly intelligent and understands human speech. It is also notoriously vengeful; grabbing one of its tails could result in a 1,000-year curse.

sections.title: Mythological Baseline
content: Based on the Kitsune of Japanese folklore—many-tailed foxes with shapeshifting and fire-breathing abilities.

sections.title: Subterranean Life
content: Diglett lives about one yard underground, where it feeds on plant roots. It only comes to the surface when it is not sunny.
Farmers often keep Diglett because it tills the earth while burrowing, leaving the soil perfectly conditioned for planting crops.
